concepts.title: Why ensembles win — the error decomposition

beats.text: Combining many weak models is the most dependable improvement in applied machine learning, and one identity explains why.
For squared error, expected test error splits exactly into three terms that cannot interfere with one another.
Bias is rigidity, variance is instability, and noise is the floor no model can ever beat.
Averaging k correlated models gives variance equal to rho times sigma squared plus the rest divided by k.
As you add members the second term vanishes, leaving the correlation floor that no amount of averaging can break.
Averaging four independent models each with variance eight gives variance two, the one over k free lunch.
Bagging is the most direct cash-out of the averaging equation: bootstrap, fit, and average high-variance learners.
Deep decision trees are the canonical base learner because they are nearly unbiased and wildly unstable.
Each bootstrap omits about thirty-seven percent of the data, the out-of-bag points that give free validation.
The omission probability is one minus one over n, raised to n, which converges to e to the minus one.
Random forests subsample features at each split, forcing weaker features in and driving member correlation down.
As trees are added the bagged variance falls roughly like one over k, then flattens at the correlation floor.
Boosting builds members in sequence, each one trained to fix the mistakes of the ensemble so far.
Gradient boosting treats the ensemble as a function optimized by gradient descent in function space.
For squared loss the pseudo-residual is literally the gap each new weak learner is fit to close.
Stagewise rounds drive the training residual norm down, with smaller learning rates needing more rounds.
AdaBoost instead reweights data, giving each weak learner a vote weighted by how well it beats chance.
A binary learner with ten percent error earns a confident vote of about two point two; a chance learner earns zero.
Stacking asks why hand-pick the combiner when a second-level meta-learner can learn the blend instead.
A meta-model takes diverse base predictions as inputs and learns which learner to trust per region.
The crucial detail is that base predictions feeding the meta-model must be out-of-fold to avoid leakage.
A diverse forest and boosted tree make different mistakes, so their stack has lower correlation than either alone.
Returns are real but modest, typically a few percent over the best single model at real operational cost.

visual.label: typical gain over best single model

beats.text: A ridge meta-learner solves for optimal weights in closed form, landing below either base learner's error.
Ensembles are robust, but the averaging equation also tells you exactly where they stop helping.

visual.sub: The limits of the free lunch

beats.text: High correlation pins variance at the floor, so the two hundredth tree adds nothing the fiftieth already gave.
Bagging a stable linear model drains little variance, so you pay a hundred times the cost for almost no gain.
Boosting on noisy labels up-weights mislabels, so train error falls to zero while test error climbs.
Stacking with in-sample predictions leaks, producing spectacular cross-validation scores that collapse in production.
Ensembling never reduces irreducible noise, and ten models can be diverse, confident, and unanimously wrong.
